skill.name: method-writing
skill.description: Write and revise the Methods section of research papers to ensure reproducibility; use when preparing an IMRAD manuscript or responding to journal/reporting-guideline requirements (e.g., CONSORT/STROBE/PRISMA).
skill.summary: Generate IMRAD-compliant Methods sections for research papers with reporting guideline adherence and statistical transparency.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 289 patterns across 5 files (2611 lines), initially scoring 100/100 risk. All 289 findings were evaluated as FALSE POSITIVES. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' hits are medical/scientific terminology (e.g., 'MD' in clinical context, hash-like reference patterns) in markdown reference documents. The 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' patterns are standard markdown code formatting (backticks for inline code and code blocks) used to denote variables, statistical terms, and example citations. The 'system/network reconnaissance' hits are scientific methodology terms in academic reference materials. The 'hardcoded URL' hits are legitimate citations to reporting guideline websites (CONSORT, STROBE, PRISMA, EQUATOR Network). The skill is a pure documentation/writing guide with no executable code, no scripts, no network requests, and no file system operations. No prompt injection attempts were detected in any skill files.

content.user_title: Write Reproducible Methods Sections for Research Papers
content.value_statement: Writing detailed Methods sections that ensure study reproducibility is difficult and time-consuming. This skill generates IMRAD-compliant Methods text with reporting guideline adherence so your manuscript meets journal and peer-review standards.
actual_capabilities: Drafts complete Methods sections in fluent paragraph-based prose with logical subsection organization
Maps methodological content to reporting checklists including CONSORT, STROBE, and PRISMA
Specifies statistical tests, effect sizes, confidence intervals, and missing-data handling in manuscript-ready language
Includes required ethical declarations, informed consent statements, and data management descriptions
Applies field-specific terminology conventions for biomedical, clinical, and laboratory research
Adapts wording, declarations, and subsection structure to match target journal author guidelines
limitations: Cannot generate actual study data, results, or findings not supported by provided source material
Output quality depends directly on the completeness and accuracy of user-provided study details
Does not replace journal-specific online submission systems or reference formatting tools
Limited to common research designs; highly specialized or novel study types may require expert adaptation

use_cases.title: First-time Author Drafting a Manuscript
use_cases.description: Receive step-by-step guidance on what each Methods subsection must contain, with concrete examples and checklist-driven completeness verification.

use_cases.title: Experienced Researcher Responding to Reviewer Feedback
use_cases.description: Check methodological completeness against reporting guidelines, identify gaps flagged by reviewers, and revise sections with precise additions.

use_cases.title: Journal Editor Screening Submissions for Completeness
use_cases.description: Map submitted Methods sections against CONSORT, STROBE, or PRISMA checklists to flag missing methodological elements before peer review.

prompt_templates.title: Draft Methods from Study Summary
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a complete Methods section for a randomized controlled trial. Study design: [design type]. Participants: [population, inclusion/exclusion criteria, sample size]. Intervention: [treatment details, duration]. Outcomes: [primary and secondary outcomes]. Statistics: [analysis plan, tests, software]. Ethics: [approval details]. Use IMRAD structure with full paragraphs and define all abbreviations at first use.
prompt_templates.scenario: You have all study details ready and need a first draft of the Methods section

prompt_templates.title: Verify Methods Against Reporting Guidelines
prompt_templates.prompt: Check the following Methods section against the CONSORT 2010 checklist. For each of the 25 CONSORT items, indicate whether the item is adequately reported and show the sentence or paragraph where it appears. Flag any missing items and suggest specific text to fill each gap. Methods section: [paste your Methods text here]
prompt_templates.scenario: You have drafted a Methods section and need to verify guideline compliance before journal submission

prompt_templates.title: Tailor Methods for a Specific Journal
prompt_templates.prompt: Adapt the following Methods section for submission to [Journal Name]. Apply the journal's formatting conventions for section headings, terminology, and required declarations. Ensure the text complies with [CONSORT/STROBE/PRISMA] as required by this journal. Original Methods section: [paste your Methods text here]
prompt_templates.scenario: You need to reformat an existing Methods section for a particular target journal

prompt_templates.title: Address Reviewer Comments on Methods
prompt_templates.prompt: Reviewers raised these concerns about my Methods section: [list reviewer comments]. For each concern, suggest specific text additions or revisions to resolve the issue. Preserve existing content that was not criticized. Ensure changes maintain CONSORT compliance and IMRAD structure. Current Methods section: [paste your text]
prompt_templates.scenario: You received reviewer feedback requesting methodological clarifications and need targeted revisions

output_examples.input: Write a Methods section for a double-blind RCT testing Drug A 50 mg vs placebo for major depressive disorder over 8 weeks. Primary outcome: change in HAM-D score. Statistics: mixed-effects model, ITT analysis, Holm correction. Ethics: IRB approved, written consent obtained.
output_examples.output: Study design and oversight. We conducted a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel-group trial over 8 weeks. Participants. Adults aged 18-65 years with a DSM-5 diagnosis of major depressive disorder and baseline HAM-D score of 20 or higher were enrolled. Randomization and blinding. Participants were assigned 1:1 to Drug A 50 mg or placebo using computer-generated block randomization with allocation concealment via a central web-based system. Outcomes. The primary outcome was change in HAM-D score from baseline to week 8, assessed by blinded raters. Statistical analysis. The primary analysis used a linear mixed-effects model following intention-to-treat principles, with Holm correction for secondary outcomes and Cohen's d effect sizes with 95% confidence intervals. Ethics. The protocol was approved by the Institutional Review Board and all participants provided written informed consent.

output_examples.input: Check this Methods section against the STROBE checklist for a cohort study. [Methods section text provided]
output_examples.output: STROBE Compliance Assessment: Items 1-3 (Title/Abstract/Introduction): Present and adequate. Item 4 (Study design): Stated but lacks rationale for cohort design choice. Item 5 (Setting): Present with dates and locations. Item 6 (Participants): Inclusion and exclusion criteria present but missing details on non-participation rate. Item 7 (Variables): Outcomes defined but confounders not fully enumerated. Item 8 (Data sources): Present with measurement methods. Items 9-11 (Bias, sample size, quantitative variables): Partially addressed. Missing: formal power calculation for sample size. Items 12-13 (Statistical methods, sensitivity): Present including adjustment for confounders, but no sensitivity analyses described. Recommendation: Add sample size justification, enumerate all confounders with their measurement methods, and include at least one sensitivity analysis.

best_practices: Provide complete study details including design type, participant criteria, interventions, outcome definitions, and statistical analysis plan before requesting Methods text
Specify which reporting guideline applies to your study and which journal you are targeting to receive correctly tailored output
Review all generated text to verify every number, procedure name, and declaration matches your actual study before manuscript submission
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill to generate findings, results, or conclusions that are not supported by your actual study data
Do not submit generated Methods text without human review and verification against your study protocol and ethics approvals
Do not apply the skill to study types or fields far outside its documented scope without expert methodological review

faq.question: What study types does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports randomized controlled trials, observational studies (cohort, case-control, cross-sectional), systematic reviews and meta-analyses, case reports, and diagnostic accuracy studies following IMRAD format.

faq.question: Can this skill write other sections of my research paper?
faq.answer: No. This skill focuses only on the Methods section. Separate skills are needed for the Introduction, Results, and Discussion sections of your manuscript.

faq.question: Which reporting guidelines does the skill cover?
faq.answer: The skill covers CONSORT for randomized trials, STROBE for observational studies, PRISMA for systematic reviews, plus SPIRIT, STARD, ARRIVE, and CARE guidelines for specialized study designs.

faq.question: What information must I provide before using this skill?
faq.answer: You need to provide your study design, participant eligibility criteria, intervention or exposure details, outcome measures with their assessment methods, and your statistical analysis approach.

faq.question: Will this skill guarantee my manuscript is accepted for publication?
faq.answer: No. The skill helps you write complete, guideline-compliant Methods text, but journal acceptance depends on your study quality, novelty, and how well it fits the journal scope.

faq.question: Does this skill run statistical analyses or generate code?
faq.answer: No. The skill describes statistical methods in manuscript-ready prose but does not write, execute, or verify statistical analysis code. You must perform your analyses separately.
